coredata.dc:title: Food waste-driven N-doped carbon dots: Applications for Fe3+ sensing and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Ahn, Jungbin
Song, Younghan
Kwon, Ji Eon
Lee, Sang Hyun
Park, Ki Soo
Kim, Seokjoon
Woo, Jeongyeon
Kim, Hyungsup
coredata.dc:description: We report highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from food waste via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. To study the chemical transition of carbon dots from food wastes, the cat feed stocks driven from food waste were used as the waste model. In the model study, the core of the CDs was successfully self N-doped without extra pre- or post-treatments. The experimental results reveal that the nitrogen in the waste model played an important role in the formation of graphitic N and pyridinic N in the core and functional groups on the surface. Especially, high process temperature (≥180 °C) resulted in high quantum yield as 23% of the CDs from the waste model. To demonstrate the conversion of real food waste into CDs, the hamburger sandwich leftover was used as a precursor for CDs. The food waste driven CDs had similar chemical and fluorescent properties to that of the waste model, having quantum yield of 28%. This study exhibits the food waste driven carbon dots are excellent candidates for fluorescence probe to Fe3+ with high selectivity even under the interference of other metal, and for bio-imaging material with good cell viability over 80%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doping
Carbon dot
Hydrothermal carbonization
Metal ion quenching
Bio-imaging
Cell viability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the CDs synthesized via HTC at (a), (d) 110 °C, (b), (e) 150 °C, and (c), (f) 180 °C, respectively. (a), (b), and (c) show the morphologies and the size distribution of the prepared CDs. (d), (e), and (f) display the CDs at the high magnification. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) spot diagrams of the CDs were inserted in (d), (e) and (f).
FT-IR spectra of the waste model and the CDs prepared by HTC at different temperatures. CD110, CD150 and CD180 referred to the CDs from 110, 150 and 180 °C, respectively.
(a) XPS survey spectra of the waste model and the CDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s and (c) N1s.

PL properties of the CDs; (a) UV–Vis spectra of the CDs synthesized at different temperatures. PL spectra of the CDs synthesized at (b) 110 °C, (c) 150 °C and (d) 180 °C. Inserted images are the fluorescent CDs irradiated by 365 nm. (e) plot of excitation wavelength versus emission wavelength of the CDs.

(a) TEM images and the fast Fourier transform (FFT) spot diagrams of the FWCDs (b) FT-IR spectra of the food waste and the FWCDs. The inserted image shows intense fluorescence of FWCDs irradiated by 365 nm.

(a) Digital images of FWCDs in distilled water and in Fe3+ solution (indicated by Fe3+) irradiated with UV light of 365 nm. (b) Plot of relative fluorescence intensity of FWCDs solution in different metal solutions (black) and metal solutions with Fe3+ (red). (c) Stern-Volmer plot of FWCDs solution at various concentrations of Fe3+, (d) Cell viability of HCT116 cells depending on the concentration of FWCDs. (e) Microscopic images along light sources by bright field and fluorescence, respectively and merged image (overlay). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of self N-doped carbon dots with multiple-functional groups synthesized via one-step hydrothermal carbonization of food waste.

The quantum yield (QY), photoluminescence lifetime (τ 1, τ 2, τ 3 and τ
av ), radiative recombination rate constant (kr) and non-radiative recombination rate constant (knr) of the CDs.

head.ce:title: Food waste-driven N-doped carbon dots: Applications for Fe3+ sensing and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Nano System Engineering, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic and Nano System Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University, Seoul 08826, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biological Engineering, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from food waste via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. To study the chemical transition of carbon dots from food wastes, the cat feed stocks driven from food waste were used as the waste model. In the model study, the core of the CDs was successfully self N-doped without extra pre- or post-treatments. The experimental results reveal that the nitrogen in the waste model played an important role in the formation of graphitic N and pyridinic N in the core and functional groups on the surface. Especially, high process temperature (≥180 °C) resulted in high quantum yield as 23% of the CDs from the waste model. To demonstrate the conversion of real food waste into CDs, the hamburger sandwich leftover was used as a precursor for CDs. The food waste driven CDs had similar chemical and fluorescent properties to that of the waste model, having quantum yield of 28%. This study exhibits the food waste driven carbon dots are excellent candidates for fluorescence probe to Fe3+ with high selectivity even under the interference of other metal, and for bio-imaging material with good cell viability over 80%.

ce:section.ce:para: Eco-friendly utilization of industrial and daily wastes, and the associated sustainable technologies are globally recognized issues in 21st century. In this regard, recycle, upcycle and conversion of wastes have attracted enormous attention in view of sustainable development and environmental protection. Among the waste of modern society, the increase of municipal waste including food left-over becomes a critical concern in many countries. The global amount of food waste already reached 1.3 billion tons in 2013 and keep increasing dramatically [1]. Recently, tremendous efforts were reported to convert the waste into fuel, energy and useful chemicals using biorefinery ways [2–5]. Among the biorefinery methods, pyrolysis produces useful gases, fuel, monomers and energy via thermal decomposition of daily and industrial wastes [6,7]. Similar to pyrolysis, various kinds of valuable chemicals can be obtained using hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) under mild processing conditions. HTC is a method to synthesize functional materials in aqueous condition from various biomasses [8,9]. Compared with pyrolysis, HTC consumes less energy and releases less air-pollutants [10,11]. During the process, organic compounds are heated, and then chemically changed at relatively low temperature (below 200 °C) under self-generated pressure in a closed container; i.e. the compounds are degraded, converted and then condensed into carbonaceous materials [8]. Depending on the process conditions and precursor types, the carbonaceous materials have various forms such as porous carbon materials, hydro-char, colloidal carbon microspheres, etc. [12–15]. In the course of purification and separation, nano-sized dots, named carbon dots (CDs) with fluorescent property can be obtained.

Carbon dot is an emerging material owing to its remarkable properties and potential uses in bio-imaging, optical sensing, photo-catalysis and photovoltaic applications [16–18]. Compared to metal or semi metal-based quantum dots, it has benefits as biocompatibility, chemical stability and low toxicity [19]. CDs are generally composed of sp2 aromatic nano-domain with abundant functional groups [20]. Due to the tunable band gap, it can emit ultra-violet (UV), visible or even near infrared fluorescence depending upon excitation wavelength [21–23]. Among the several methods, nitrogen-doping (N-doping) has been widely studied to improve the fluorescence of carbon dots [24–26]. Generally, there are two approaches to construct effective N-doped core. One is acid-treatment of carbon dots after HTC and the other is hydrothermal carbonization of organic precursor with extra N-sources [27,28].

In this study, we directly synthesized N-doped CDs without extra pre- or post-treatment via HTC of food waste. The prepared CDs showed strong fluorescence with high quantum yield of 28%. Furthermore, the food waste driven CDs could be used as fluorescence probe for Fe3+ sensing with high selectivity. It also had excellent cell-viability in the high CDs concentration and good bio-imaging performance.

Cat feed stocks (Catsrang, Dajoo industry, Republic of Korea) produced from the organic waste was used as a waste model for the synthesis of CDs. The feed stocks contain 34% (w/w dry matter) crude protein, 18% (w/w dry matter) crude fat, 1.5% (w/w dry matter) crude fiber and 10% (w/w dry matter) crude ash. Before hydrothermal carbonization, the feed stocks were ground to fine powder and dried at 65 °C for 24 h. The dried powder was mixed with 50 ml distilled water for 7 wt%. The mixture was poured and sealed in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and hydrothermally carbonized at 110, 150, and 180 °C for 24 h. The autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. After filtration of the treated solution using PTFE syringe filter with pore size of 0.5 μm, dialysis was carried out using dialysis bag (Biotech CE dialysis tubing, 0.5–1.0 kDa, Spectrum Labs., USA). The solution was stored at 1 °C until further characterizations. Food waste-driven carbon dots (FWCDs) were hydrothermally carbonized at 180 °C using hamburger sandwich leftover (See Figure 3 in [40]).

AlCl3∙6H2O, Pb(NO)2, AgNO3, FeCl3∙6H2O, FeCl2∙4H2O, CaCl2, HgCl2, ZnCl2, CuCl2∙H2O and MgCl2∙6H2O were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA) and used for the metal ion detection test. Metal ion selectivity of FWCDs was tested at room temperature in each metal ion aqueous solution. All metal salts were dissolved in distilled water at concentration of 500 μM. 10 μg/ml of FWCDs were uniformly dispersed in each metal aqueous solution. After 30 min of adding the FWCDs in each metal solutions, the fluorescence intensities were measured by excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Limit of detection (LOD) was determined by LOD = 3σ/K. σ is defined as the standard deviation of the blank signal of (I0 – I) / I0 values. K represents the slope at the linear relationships in the range of 12.5 to 100 μM.

The cytotoxicity of FWCDs was assessed by 3‑(4,5‑dimethylthizaol‑2‑yl)‑2,5‑dyphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Human colorectal carcinoma HCT116 cells were incubated in a 96-well plate at a density of 10,000 cells per each well for 24 h. After the extracellular medium was removed, FWCDs solutions with concentration range of 5 to 500 μg/ml were added to every well and cultured for 24 h. The cells were further incubated for 4 h with a solution of MTT. The cells without FWCDs treatment were considered as control cells and were used as a reference for the relative survival of the cells incubated with FWCDs.

Cell viabilities of each sample were calculated from a following equation where As stand for the sample absorbance, Ac for the controlled cell absorbance and Ab is the absorbance of the blank culture media. The all absorbance was read at 595 nm measured by UV–Vis spectrometer. The tests were repeated for 3 times.
% cell viability =
A s
−
A b
A c
−
A b
× 100

Cellular images were taken to confirm the suitability of FWCDs for bio-applications. HCT116 cells were seeded in cell culture dish and incubated for 24 h. Then the cells were treated with 500 μg/ml of FWCDs at 37 °C for another 4 h. The supernatant was carefully removed, and the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution (pH 7.2–7.4, BIOSESANG, Republic of Korea).

The morphologies of the prepared CDs were confirmed using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific, U.K.) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-alpha, Thermo Scientific, U·K) were carried out to characterize the chemical structure of the samples. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the CDs were studied using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (Cary 60 UV/vis spectrophotometer, Agilent Technologies, USA) and PL spectroscopy (FS-2, SICNCO, Republic of Korea). Fluorescence lifetimes were obtained by the time-correlated single photon counting method (TCSPC, Fluo Time 200 instrument, Picoquant, Germany). A 342 nm pulsed LED with repetition rate of 5 MHz was applied as an excitation source. The decay profiles were analysed by FluoFit Pro software using exponential fitting models through deconvolution with instrumental response functions (IRF). The cell images were taken with fluorescence microscopy (RX50, Yenway, China) with the excitation filter of DAPI.

Scheme 1 illustrates the one-step synthesis of self N-doped CDs with the multi-functional groups via HTC of the food waste. Cat feed stocks produced from food waste was used as the waste model for the CDs. To demonstrate the conversion of real food waste into CDs, the hamburger sandwich leftover was used as a precursor for CDs. The HTC was conducted under three different temperatures (110, 150 and 180 °C) without extra additives or treatments.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the morphologies and the fast Fourier transform spot diagrams of the prepared CDs were observed using TEM. The CDs carbonized at 150 and 180 °C had spherical shape with diameter of 2–5 nm (Fig. 1b and c), while the CDs treated at 110 °C had large fused sphere (Fig. 1a). The CDs from 150 and 180 °C had clear lattice fringes with 0.21 nm (corresponding to in-plane lattice spacing of the (100) graphene facet) [29] and 0.36 nm (corresponding to the (002) graphite facet) [30], respectively (Fig. 1e and f). However, the lattice fringe was hardly observed for the CDs from 110 °C even for the CDs smaller than 10 nm (Fig. 1d). It confirms that condensed graphite structure could be developed successfully at higher temperature.

The FT-IR spectra of the waste model and the synthesized CDs are shown in Fig. 2 and the characteristic peaks are summarized in Table 1 in [40]. In the spectrum, the waste model contained the typical characteristic peaks of saccharide, protein and fats [31–33]. The chemical structure of the prepared sample was dramatically changed even at temperature of 110 °C. By the hydrothermal carbonization, the peaks for fatty acid, protein and saccharide were diminished and the peaks for OH, NH (3400–3200 cm−1), CN (1400 cm−1), and CO (1050 cm−1) vibration were observed more clearly [23,34,35]. The waste model was significantly decomposed and condensed to carbonaceous structure [8]. As the process temperature increased, the peaks for the absorption at –NH stretching, amide I, amide II and CH out-of-plane bending (phenazine skeleton) became more distinct [23,36,37]. For temperature over 150 °C, the nitrogen-containing aromatic structures (graphitic N and pyridinic N) were more developed, which was discussed again in XPS later.

The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3 a) display that the all samples had the peaks around 284.4, 399.5 and 532 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The hydrothermally carbonized dots had the larger nitrogen contents at the higher process temperature. The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 3b) contain the peaks of CC/CC (284.7 eV), CN (285.3 eV), CO (286.4 eV), CO/CN (287.7 eV), and COOH (289 eV) [38]. As similar to the FT-IR results, the peaks at 285.3 and 287.7 eV got larger with increase of temperature, indicating that the chemical state of the hetero-atom became more predominant in the form of CN and CN/CO. These changes were also observed in the N1 s spectra (Fig. 3c). The N1 s spectra contain peaks of pyridinic N (398.8 eV), amino N (399.6 eV), pyrrolic N (400.3 eV) and graphitic N (401.2 eV) [38]. The waste model in the study contains amino N and pyrrolic N from protein [39]. The N1 s speciation of the CDs illustrates the amino N and the pyrrolic N were converted to pyridinic N and graphitic N. The ratios of the pyridinic N and the graphitic N against total N speciation were increased with temperature (see Fig. 1(b) in [40]). It indicates more nitrogen was introduced into the core of CDs, i.e. the self N-doped CDs.

Fig. 4 shows the UV–vis absorption and the PL spectra of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 4a, the all absorption curves were decreased along the wavelength regardless of the HTC temperature. The long tail in the absorption curve of the CDs produced at 110 °C was mainly attributed to Mie scattering by the large fused sphere, as shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1a) [41]. On the contrast, the CDs obtained at higher than 150 °C had the absorption peak around 270 nm, which is attributed to π → π* transition of aromatic ring structure [21,42,43]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs from 180 °C showed stronger shoulder absorption around 325 nm corresponding to n → π* transition [20,43,44]. It was resulted from the edge states on the boundary of the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons and the electron transition of the functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and amide as discussed in the FT-IR section.

Fig. 4b, c and d are the PL spectra of the CDs synthesized at the different temperatures with the inserted image of fluorescent CDs irradiated by 365 nm. The CDs illuminated sky blue and it became brighter with the increase of the process temperature. However, the illumination was negligible for the CDs obtained at 110 °C. The all emission spectra exhibit the excitation-dependent emission along the wavelength [45,46]. This excitation-dependent emission is arising from π* → n transitions of the multiple functionalities such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and amide, confirmed by the FT-IR spectrum [23].

For the UV excitation of 340 nm, the CDs obtained at 110 and 150 °C had the emission spectra with the maximum fluorescence peaks at 425 and 427 nm, respectively. Whereas, the maximum emission peak for the CDs produced at 180 °C was red-shift to 435 nm (excited by UV of 360 nm). The red-shift was originated from the change of N-doped core content. As described in Figs. 1–3, the CDs from 180 °C had dense core with more graphitic N and pyridinic N comparing to CDs from 110 and 150 °C, while the functional groups were not significantly changed along the temperature. The larger content of N-doped core gave more chance to endows the N related impurity energy levels below the π* energy level resulting in lower energy emission [47,48].

Fig. 4e shows the relationship between the excitation wavelength versus the emission wavelength of the CDs. As described above, the all CDs have red-shifted emission peaks in the range from 320 to 420 nm of the excitation wavelength. On the contrast, the emission peaks were blue-shifted when the given excitation wavelengths were smaller than 320 nm. The discrete absorption energy levels driven from the developed graphitic core allow electrons to be excited to the higher energy level [49]. The excited electrons could be effectively relaxed to the ground state via radiative recombination and resulted in lower emission wavelength. At the excitation wavelength of 320 nm, the geometric symmetry was well-matched with the lowest emission wavelength, showing the smallest stokes shift [50].

The absolute fluorescence quantum yield and the fluorescence decays of the CDs were characterized at the emission wavelength of the individual maximum intensity (Table 1 ). The fluorescence decay curves of the all CDs were fitted by the tri-exponential decay kinetics (see Fig. 2 in [40]). The radiative and non-radiative recombination rate constants (kr and knr) were calculated using the relationship between the quantum yield and the average lifetime. The used equations are expressed in [40] (Eqs. (2), (3) and (4)). The quantum yield was increased from 17 to 23% as the process temperature increased from 150 to 180 °C, while the quantum yield of the CDs from 110 °C couldn't be obtained due to its low emission intensity. The enhancement of the quantum yield can be interpreted by the composition of the N1s speciation. As described in the XPS, the total contents of the pyridinic N and the graphitic N were increased up to 27% for the CDs from 180 °C. Compared with the CDs from 150 °C, the contents of the pyrrolic N was decreased from 47 to 31%. The N-doped core domain of the graphitic N and pyridinic N as well as the multi-functional groups of the CDs from 180 °C endows more extra electrons to π system [51]. As a result, the CD from 180 °C had more excited electrons at π* energy level than those of 110 and 150 °C. Moreover, the decrease of pyrrolic N reduced the in-planarity of the sp2 carbon nano domain, and in turns changed the non-radiative rate constant from 28.8 to 23.3 (×107 s−1) [52,53]. Consequently, the electrons had more chance to be stabilized to the ground state via the radiative recombination, enhancing the quantum yield as 23%.

The hamburger sandwich leftover was hydrothermally carbonized at 180 °C to compare the properties and the chemical structure of the CDs with the CDs from the waste model (see Fig. 3 in [40]). Fig. 5 shows the characterized results of the FWCDs. The FWCDs were successfully synthesized to spherical shaped particle with the size of 2–4 nm. It also had the lattice fringes with 0.21 nm corresponding to in-plane lattice spacing of the graphene (100) facet [28]. The FT-IR spectra of the food waste and the FWCDs in Fig. 5b showed almost identical to the spectra of the waste model. The quantum yield of the FWCDs reached 28% with bright sky-blue emission.

Fig. 6 a is the digital images of emitted FWCDs in distilled water and Fe3+ aqueous solution of 500 μM, respectively. As shown in the figure, the fluorescence was dramatically quenched. The fluorescence intensity ratio of the FWCDs solutions in the presence (I) against the absence (I0) of various metal ions are shown in Fig. 4a in [40]. The fluorescence intensities of the FWCDs slightly decreased in the all metal solutions. Comparing with the other metal ions, Fe3+ decreased the fluorescence intensity remarkably. The hydroxyl and amine groups on FWCDs could be easily coordinated with Fe3+, resulting in higher selectivity toward Fe3+ [54,55]. For the practical application of FWCDs for Fe3+ sensing, Fe3+ was mixed with the other metal ions (Fe3+: metal ion = 1: 1) studied in the experiment (see Fig. 6b). The fluorescence emission response of the mixed solution was measured in the same way. Even in the presence of other metal ions, the fluorescence was significantly decreased by Fe3+, while the fluorescence quenching by other metal ions did not show dramatic quenching. The fluorescence of FWCDs was selectively quenched to Fe3+ without interference by other metal ions. The sensitivity of FWCDs toward Fe3+ was evaluated by plotting (I0 – I) / I0 versus Fe3+ concentration range of 0–500 μM (Fig. 6c). The plot showed linear relationship and LOD was calculated as 32 μM.

For bio-application of food waste-driven carbon dots, the cell viability of HCT116 cells was calculated from the in-vitro test for different FWCDs concentrations (Fig. 6d). Even at the high FWCDs concentration of 500 μg/ml, the cell viability was higher than 80%. It indicates that the food waste-driven carbon dots in this study have insignificant toxicity and are suitable for bio-applications including bio-imaging. Fig. 6e shows the fluorescence images of HCT116 cells treated with 500 μg/ml of FWCDs for 4 h. The cells maintained their original shapes and exhibited bright fluorescence of blue under UV excitation. There was no negative effect on the cell survivability even when the FWCDs penetrated the cell.

By the one-step hydrothermal carbonization of food waste, we directly obtained the carbon dots with N-doped core and multi-functional groups on the surface, resulting in strong fluorescent properties. At high temperature of HTC, the nitrogen was more effectively introduced as graphitic N and pyridinic N into the core and resulted in self N-doping. Both the self N-doped core and the multiple functional groups in the food waste-driven CDs led extraordinary fluorescent performance with high quantum yield of 28%. The PL of the food waste-driven CDs was quenched by Fe3+ even in the presence of other metal ions. It can be applied to fluorescence detection probe for Fe3+ with high selectivity. The excellent cell viability higher than 80% (at 500 μg/ml of CDs) leads the FWCDs for toxicity free bioimaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science (NRF-2018R1A2B6003570). This paper was supported by Konkuk University Researcher Fund in 2018.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J. Ahn, Y. Song, J. E. Kwon, J. Woo, H. Kim, Data in brief, submitted, Characterization of Food Waste-Driven Carbon Dot Focusing on Chemical Structural, Electron Relaxation Behavior and Fe3+ Selective Sensing.
